guideline_references: Consortium for Spinal Cord Medicine — Acute Management of Autonomic Dysreflexia (Clinical Practice Guideline)
ASIA / ISCoS International Standards for Neurological Classification of Spinal Cord Injury
intentional_test_features: Diagnostic overshadowing trap: a patient with a lifelong disability presents with new acute symptoms (pounding headache, sweating, 'panic') that must be investigated as a treatable medical emergency (autonomic dysreflexia from a blocked catheter) rather than dismissed as anxiety.

Anchoring / red-herring: severe headache + very high blood pressure invites premature closure on 'hypertensive crisis' or 'migraine'; the discriminating features (reflex bradycardia, sharp autonomic demarcation at the lesion level, a noxious stimulus below the lesion) point to autonomic dysreflexia.

Telehealth reprojection: the source note is an in-person clinician who palpated the bladder and physically unkinked the catheter; the AI Doctor cannot examine or unkink the tube itself and is scored on recognising autonomic dysreflexia, coaching the carer through the first-aid steps a carer CAN do (sit fully upright, loosen clothing, trace and unkink the catheter tubing / relieve the noxious stimulus), and escalating to 000 for a hypertensive emergency.

Baseline T4 spinal cord injury, wheelchair dependence, indwelling suprapubic catheter and neurogenic bladder/bowel are KNOWN lifelong conditions the carer legitimately states as history; the sealed answer is the NEW acute complication (autonomic dysreflexia crisis) and the correct emergency triage.
Adult patient — no paediatric dosing involved; the note's PRN emergency antihypertensive is prescribed but no dose is stated, so no dose is synthesised.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
